name: autolycus (master thief)
shortDescription: Autolycus was a celebrated trickster and master thief in Greek mythology, known for his cunning and ability to change the appearance of stolen goods. He was the son of Hermes and Chione.
description: Autolycus, in Greek mythology, was the son of Hermes, the messenger god, and Chione, daughter of Daedalion. He inherited his father's cunning and skill, becoming renowned as the most skilled thief of his time. Autolycus possessed the power to change the appearance of anything he stole, making it virtually impossible to trace the stolen goods back to him. This ability made him infamous and highly sought after, both for his services and for his misdeeds. He is a significant figure in several myths, most notably as the grandfather of Odysseus. It was Autolycus who gave Odysseus his name, meaning 'trouble' or 'giver of pain,' after the infant was wounded during a boar hunt. Autolycus is also credited with teaching Heracles (Hercules) the art of wrestling. His cunning and thievery often led to conflicts and adventures, solidifying his reputation as a legendary trickster and a key figure in the genealogies of several prominent heroes. While not typically worshipped with temples or dedicated rituals like the Olympian gods, Autolycus represents the darker, more chaotic side of divine influence and the ever-present theme of deception in Greek myth.
